summarization.a: Si sample after 1000 Ar⁺ impacts at 20 eV. The top layers are amorphized but the crystalline bulk is largely preserved. Some fluorocarbon species remain at the surface.
summarization.b: Si sample after 1000 Ar⁺ impacts at 40 eV. The amorphized region is significantly thicker and extends deeper into the substrate due to higher ion penetration depth.

b.question: How does the amorphization depth differ between 20 and 40 eV Ar⁺ bombardment?
b.answer: The amorphized layer is thicker at 40 eV because higher energy ions penetrate deeper into the sample.

b.question: Based on this figure, is 20 or 40 eV preferable for minimizing substrate damage?
b.answer: 20 eV, as it produces a thinner amorphized region.

a.question: What do the dark spheres represent in this figure?
Why is substrate amorphization a concern for layer-by-layer etching?
a.answer: Amorphization disrupts the crystalline order of the substrate, which can degrade device performance in applications requiring high-quality crystalline interfaces. It also affects subsequent etching cycles by changing the surface structure and bonding environment.
